posts.title: 48 Hours in Kyoto
posts.excerpt: Two days is not enough. But it's enough to understand why people come back.
posts.content:
# 48 Hours in Kyoto

I arrived on a Tuesday morning in March, which turned out to be accidental genius. The cherry blossoms were two weeks away, the tour groups hadn't fully mobilised, and the city had a quietness that I don't think it gets in peak season.

My hotel was a 10-minute walk from Gion. I dropped my bag and left immediately.

## The First Morning: Fushimi Inari

Everyone goes to Fushimi Inari. Everyone is right to go to Fushimi Inari. The trick is to go early — I was at the trailhead by 6:45am — and to keep walking past the point where most tourists turn around.

The lower gates are dramatic. But the higher you climb, the thinner the crowds get, and somewhere around the 2-hour mark you find yourself alone in a tunnel of orange torii that seems to go on forever. The silence is unexpected. So is the effort — it's a proper hike, not a stroll.

I came back down in time to buy coffee from a vending machine at the base, which cost ¥120 and was perfect.

## The Afternoon: Getting Deliberately Lost in Gion

Gion is one of those neighbourhoods that rewards aimlessness. The main drag, Hanamikoji Street, is photogenic and crowded. The side streets — Shinbashi, the lanes behind it — are where the city gets interesting.

I walked for three hours. I saw a machiya townhouse being converted into something. A woman in a kimono turning quickly into a doorway. A cat sleeping in a window above a tea shop that had no English sign and no menu I could read. I pointed at what the person next to me was having. It was good.

> There's a particular kind of travel tiredness that isn't unpleasant — you're full of things you saw, your feet hurt, and you don't want the day to end.

That's what Kyoto afternoons feel like.

## Day Two: Arashiyama and the Bamboo Grove

The bamboo grove photographs have been reproduced so many times that you expect to be disappointed in person. You won't be. There's something about the scale and density of it — the way the stalks creak, the quality of light filtering through — that no image captures.

I spent the morning in Arashiyama and found myself at Tenryu-ji garden by accident, which turned out to be the highlight of both days. A Zen garden designed in the 14th century, still intact, with Arashiyama mountain as its borrowed scenery. I sat on the veranda for 40 minutes and didn't feel like I'd wasted them.

## What I'd Do Differently

I'd book a restaurant. Kyoto has some of the best food in Japan and I ate well, but the places I most wanted to try had no walk-in availability. Next time I'll plan at least one proper dinner in advance.

I'd also allow three days, not two. The city reveals itself slowly. Two days is enough to fall in love with it. Three might be enough to start understanding it.

---

*Kyoto is a three-hour shinkansen ride from Tokyo. A JR Pass makes this straightforward if you're already travelling Japan. March and November are the classic seasons — for good reason.*

posts.title: My Remote Dev Setup for Long Trips
posts.excerpt: What's actually in the bag after three years of working from airports, cafés, and the occasional very slow train.
posts.content:
# My Remote Dev Setup for Long Trips

I've been working remotely while travelling seriously for about three years. In that time I've gone from carrying too much to carrying too little, and I've settled somewhere in the middle that I'm actually happy with.

This isn't a gear review post. It's more an honest account of what I've found matters and what I thought would matter but doesn't.

## The Non-Negotiables

### A laptop that doesn't make you miserable

I use a MacBook Pro 14". Not because Apple is the only answer, but because the battery life and the display are genuinely excellent for the price point, and I've never had a hardware failure on a Mac mid-trip. That last part matters more than people think — the anxiety of working on ageing hardware is a real productivity drain.

Get the best machine you can justify. You'll use it every day for years.

### A portable monitor

This was my biggest quality-of-life upgrade. I use a 15" portable USB-C monitor that weighs about 800g. When I'm in an apartment for more than a few days, having two screens is the difference between working well and working poorly.

For short stops or when weight matters, I leave it behind. But for stays over a week, it comes.

### A real keyboard and mouse

I carry a compact mechanical keyboard and a small trackball. Your wrists will thank you. Hotel desks are the ergonomic equivalent of a park bench — a proper keyboard adds about 400g but removes the low-grade discomfort that accumulates over long sessions.

## The Software Side

The hardware is honestly the easy part. The software setup is where things get interesting.

```bash
# The first thing I run on any new machine
brew install git node python ripgrep fzf tmux
```

### Terminal-first wherever possible

I've moved almost everything I can to the terminal. Not for ideological reasons — because it travels better. VS Code with 12 extensions behaves differently on different machines. Neovim with my config in a dotfiles repo is identical everywhere.

If you haven't committed your dotfiles to a git repo, do it today. It's a one-time hour of work that pays back every time you set up a new machine.

### VPN

Non-negotiable in countries with unreliable or restricted internet. I use Mullvad. Paid, audited, no-logs. Worth it.

### Offline-first thinking

I plan for connection problems rather than assuming good internet. That means:

- Working locally as much as possible and pushing when connected
- Downloading documentation, not bookmarking it
- Using `git pull` as a habit before I leave anywhere with good WiFi

> The best internet you'll have on a trip is usually the hotel at check-in. Use it.

## What I Thought I Needed But Don't

**A travel router.** Tried it. Added complexity, rarely needed.

**A Starlink subscription.** Overkill unless you're genuinely in the middle of nowhere for extended periods.

**Multiple backup devices.** One good machine, backed up properly with Time Machine and a cloud sync, is enough. Two mediocre machines is not better than one good one.

## The Thing Nobody Talks About

Time zones. They are the actual hard part of remote work while travelling.

When I'm in Europe and my team is in San Francisco, there's a 9-hour gap. I've made peace with this by being explicit about async communication — I write longer, more complete messages, I document decisions, and I protect a 2-hour overlap window in the morning where I'm available synchronously.

The teams I've worked with have all adjusted. The ones who haven't are the ones with a culture of expecting instant responses to Slack messages, which is a separate problem regardless of time zones.

---

*The bag, if you're curious: Tom Bihn Synik 30. It fits under a plane seat, meets carry-on requirements everywhere I've been, and I've used it for trips from 2 days to 3 months.*

posts.title: The Case for Slow Travel
posts.excerpt: Seven countries in three weeks sounds impressive. It isn't.
posts.content:
# The Case for Slow Travel

In 2019 I did a trip that I was proud of at the time: seven countries in twenty-two days. I have photographs from all of them. I remember fragments — a canal in Amsterdam, a square in Prague, a bus station in Budapest at 4am. I don't remember much else.

I've been thinking about why.

## The Checklist Problem

Travel organised around a list of places produces a specific kind of experience. You arrive, you orient yourself, you see the main things, you leave. Each destination gets the same 36-48 hours. You become efficient at being a tourist.

The problem isn't the places. The problem is the mode. When you're always arriving or departing, you never actually land anywhere. You're permanently in transit — physically present but mentally elsewhere, already calculating the next connection.

I think this is why I remember fragments. Fragments are what you collect when you're moving too fast to collect anything deeper.

## What Changes When You Slow Down

I spent six weeks in Iceland two years ago. Six weeks in one country that most people visit for a week.

By week two I had a favourite coffee shop. By week three I'd driven the same stretch of Route 1 four times in different weather and understood, finally, why landscape photographers come back year after year. By week four I'd made friends — actual ones, not the transient hostel kind — and was being invited to things that weren't on any itinerary.

> The best things that happened to me in Iceland weren't planned. They couldn't have been. You can't plan your way into the texture of a place — you have to wait for it.

Slow travel isn't about seeing less. It's about the difference between reading the first chapter of twelve books and actually finishing one.

## The Practical Objection

The obvious pushback is time. Most people don't have six weeks.

Fair. But the principle scales. Two weeks in one country is better than two weeks across four. One week in one city, really in it, beats the highlights tour. Even a long weekend spent without an itinerary will give you something a packed schedule won't.

The question isn't whether you have enough time to go slowly. It's whether you're willing to trade quantity for depth.

## What I'm Doing Now

I plan fewer places. I stay longer when I get there. I allow for days with no plan at all, which used to make me anxious and now feels like the whole point.

The seven-countries-in-three-weeks version of me would look at my current trip log and think I wasn't travelling enough. He'd be wrong. He was collecting passport stamps. I'm collecting something harder to photograph.

---

*None of this is new. Travel writers have been making this argument since long before Instagram made the checklist problem worse. But it took me a few years of doing it wrong to understand why they were right.*
